feedback.text: As a game developer, I completely agree: games shouldn't just vanish. When you buy a game, especially one you love, you should be able to play it for years to come, even if the company behind it decides to stop supporting it. This isn't just about preserving fun; it's about respecting the players who spent their hard-earned money. Many modern games rely on online servers to function, even for single-player content. When a publisher decides to "kill" a game, they often shut down these servers. This can make the game completely unplayable, turning your purchase into worthless digital dust. It's like buying a physical board game, only to have the company come to your house and take it away years later. "The Crew" and "Lawbreakers" are painful examples of this. Players paid for these games, invested their time, and then suddenly, they were gone. In the case of "Lawbreakers," it feels like the creators just took the money and ran, leaving players with nothing. It doesn't have to be this way. Game developers can, and should, think about a game's "end of life" from the very beginning. This means making sure that even if the online features are eventually shut down, the core game remains playable offline. "Need for Speed: ProStreet" is a shining example. Even after its official servers went offline, you could still play the main game offline. Plus, a clever update allowed players to host their own private multiplayer games, effectively preserving the multiplayer experience forever. This shows that with a little foresight, games can live on for decades. The idea that a game might disappear can actually make it more addictive. When you know a game has an expiration date, you might feel pressured to play more, to get all the items, or to experience everything before it's too late. This "fear of missing out" (FOMO) can be a powerful driver, pushing players to spend more time and even money on a game than they normally would. For many, this time could be better spent on other productive activities, like, say, developing their own games! As a developer, I believe in making games that last. It's not difficult to design a game with its long-term future in mind. If a game is primarily multiplayer, providing players with the tools to host their own servers is a great solution. This ensures that even if the original company moves on, players can continue to enjoy the game with their friends for years to come. Ultimately, games are a form of art and entertainment, and they should be preserved. We, as developers, have a responsibility to our players to ensure that the experiences we create can be enjoyed for as long as people want to play them.
